Kids&Us

Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Thomas
  • Teacher: Clarisse Bydens
  • Level: Sam Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Thomas usually came to class in a good mood and showed interest in the activities. He particularly seemed to enjoy the "Let's go to the zoo" activity, the "Why are you so sad?" story and the routines. However, he was sometimes a bit uncooperative and needed reminders to stay involved and participate with the group. At times, he had to be encouraged to join in with the class, but when focused, he was able to follow along well.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Thomas's English knowledge is good. He can understand and repeat the vocabulary and structures worked on in class. He was able to tell the colours, the genders and the moods, and he could also repeat the weather and do the gestures for it. He could count very well and matched the dot cards with the correct numbers successfully. He sometimes needed a little help to remember the full structure, but he showed a good understanding of the content and could respond well when supported.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas is a quiet student who can be attentive and cooperative when he is engaged in the lesson. He sometimes needed to be accompanied more closely into the activities and was not always willing to participate with the group. On one occasion, he did not respect the classroom materials and needed several reminders to handle them properly. Nevertheless, he was able to show his understanding during the activities and, when focused, he worked well with the teacher and the class.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 9.00 Very good. Thomas could do the test very well and showed a good understanding of the vocabulary and structures.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Thomas has shown that he can understand and use the language well, especially when he is focused and willing to take part. He has made good progress in his oral comprehension and in his ability to repeat the structures learnt in class. It would be beneficial to continue listening to the audio track at home in order to keep in mind the vocabulary words and structures learnt throughout the school year. The teacher would like to encourage Thomas to keep improving his participation and consistency in class.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the examples. The notes include absences and mixed behaviour — the report should handle these accurately without inventing positive observations. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - absences: absent sessions noted correctly, not fabricated into positive observations - tone: warm but honest, matches examples "}]

[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: Informes 3r trimestre Lou-Ellie Colas Acceptable Sam.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Lou-Ellie Colas Teacher: Clarisse Bydens Level: Sam 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Pass Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good Comment: Lou-Ellie usually comes to class in a happy, easy-going mood. She takes part in most activities with great enthusiasm. She loves story time and even plays an active role turning the pages of the storybook and sometimes recites the story with the teacher. She also enjoys the "Ants/Bugs counting", the "Have you got Sam's sandwich?", the "Bugs on a picnic" and the "Actions TPR" activities. She is usually quite cooperative with her teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Lou-Ellie's English knowledge is good. She knows a decent amount of vocabulary and structures. She can sometimes use them in sentences, often requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, she specifically learnt the different bugs and the different picnic items. She has also reinforced her knowledge of the sizes, the colours, the moods, the types of weather and the genders. She can also count quite well. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lou-Ellie is an easy-going, good-humoured, enthusiastic student. She treats the teacher with respect and care. She does sometimes get a bit distracted by external elements and needs to be reminded to stay focused and listen to the teacher. She generally does so without a problem and follows the teacher's instructions very well. She takes good care of the material and gives it back directly when asked. Marks Oral Test Mark: 7.67 Acceptable. --- Final comment Throughout the school year, Lou-Ellie has mostly been coming to class in a cheerful, happy mood. She has been getting better at staying focused during most of the lesson. She has also made progress in her learning, developing both her memory and her comprehension skills. Therefore, it is recommended to listen to the audio track during the summer holidays in order to keep in mind the vocabulary words and structures learnt throughout the school year. ### Example 2: Informes 3r trimestre Lina Carboni Very Good Sam.pdf Kids&Us Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Lina Carboni Teacher: Clarisse Bydens Level: Sam 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Lina usually comes to class in a happy mood and a huge smile on her face. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lina is a joyful, fun-loving and good-humoured student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with care and respect. She generally listens attentively to the teacher and stays focused during the activities, usually following the teacher's instructions very well. She gives the material back when asked, she shares it with her classmates and she takes good care of it without a problem. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.80 Very good. --- Final comment At the beginning of the school year, Lina was a quiet student who was always in a good mood, but needed to be encouraged to participate in the activities. Now, she has found her place in her group. She is also very motivated to take part in the various activities. Moreover, she has made great progress in her English learning, developing both her memory and comprehension skills. Therefore, it is recommended to listen to the audio track during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary words and structures learnt throughout the school year. ### Example 3: Informes 3r trimestre Arthur Motte Good Sam.pdf Kids&Us Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Arthur Motte Teacher: Clarisse Bydens Level: Sam 2024/2025 Absences: 5 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Arthur usually comes to class in a quiet, yet happy mood. Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Thomas
Teacher: [Not present in notes]
Level: Sam Saturday
Absences: [Not present in notes]
Frequency of use (audio): [Not present in notes]

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Thomas was attentive and did what was asked of him on occasion. He showed understanding of vocabulary such as moods, genders, and weather, and could perform the gestures for the weather. However, he was not overly participative at times and needed encouragement to engage fully in the group routines. He enjoyed the activities, particularly the 'Why are you so sad' story, where he could repeat phrases well.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Thomas demonstrated a solid grasp of vocabulary and structures. He could tell moods, genders, and weather, and could count very well. He paired numbers with spots effectively and repeated phrases accurately. However, he needed help to recall the full structure at times and required assistance to identify animals. His performance in the test was strong, though he did not use gestures like the others during the "Let's go to the zoo" activity.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Thomas generally followed instructions and participated in activities. He treated the material with care but, at times, required reminders to follow group norms, such as not bending cards. He was cooperative in most tasks but showed some resistance in specific moments, such as refusing to high-five after repeated reminders about card handling.

Final comment

Thomas has shown steady progress in his learning, particularly in vocabulary recall and participation. While he sometimes needed encouragement to fully engage, his enthusiasm for activities like story time and counting games was evident. Continued practice with the audio tracks would help reinforce the vocabulary and structures learned this term.

componentResults.reason: The output follows the required three-section structure plus a final comment, with assessments and comments for each section. Most statements are grounded in the notes: Thomas’s vocabulary knowledge, counting, weather gestures, inconsistent participation, needing help with animals/full structures, needing to be carried to routines, and refusing to high-five are all traceable. However, it does not handle absences explicitly and partially attributes enjoyment of the 'Why are you so sad' story to Thomas even though one note about loving that story says Thomas was absent; this is only partly supported by another note saying he could repeat the phrase. The tone is warm but somewhat more negative than the style examples, and the assessments of Participation/Learning as 'Acceptable' are stricter than the benchmark, though not impossible from the notes. Overall it is structurally sound and mostly grounded, with some issues around absence handling and a few overstatements.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Thomas
  • Class: Sam Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas shows moments of attentiveness and understanding, particularly when working with vocabulary like moods, genders, and weather. He enjoys certain activities, such as the "Why are you so sad" story, and can repeat phrases well when engaged. However, his participation is inconsistent—he can be uncooperative at times and requires encouragement to join group routines. While he demonstrates enthusiasm in some lessons, he occasionally resists interaction, such as refusing to high-five the teacher after a reminder about handling materials.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas has a good grasp of foundational vocabulary, including colours, genders, moods, and weather terms. He can repeat weather phrases and perform the corresponding gestures, and he counts well, successfully pairing numbers with spots. However, he struggles with some structures, needing help to recall full phrases or identify animals independently. His knowledge of zoo-related vocabulary and the "Let's go to the zoo" activity is less consistent, as he did not always follow along with gestures like his peers. His comprehension improves with support, and he shows progress in repeating and applying phrases when guided.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas is generally cooperative when focused but can be unresponsive or resistant at times. He required physical guidance to join group routines and, along with a classmate, refused to follow a simple social gesture (high-fiving) after a reminder about material care. While he engages well in activities he enjoys, such as story time, his behaviour can be inconsistent—sometimes attentive and other times disengaged. He benefits from clear instructions and reminders to stay on task.

---

Final comment

Thomas has shown flashes of strong comprehension, particularly with core vocabulary like moods, genders, and weather, and he excels in counting and matching tasks. His participation and behaviour, however, remain uneven—he is capable of focus and cooperation but sometimes needs encouragement or redirection. To build consistency, it would be helpful to reinforce routines and positive interactions. Reviewing the audio materials over the break could also support his retention of vocabulary and structures, especially those he finds challenging, like animal names and full phrases. With continued practice, Thomas has the potential to grow more confident and engaged in class.

Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Teacher: Clarisse Bydens

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Acceptable

He sometimes needed to be accompanied more closely into the activities and was not always willing to participate with the group. On one occasion, he did not respect the classroom materials and needed several reminders to handle them properly. Nevertheless, he was able to show his understanding during the activities and, when focused, he worked well with the teacher and the class.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 9.00 Very good. Thomas could do the test very well and showed a good understanding of the vocabulary and structures.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Thomas has shown that he can understand and use the language well, especially when he is focused and willing to take part. He has made good progress in his oral comprehension and in his ability to repeat the structures learnt in class. It would be beneficial to continue listening to the audio track at home in order to keep in mind the vocabulary words and structures learnt throughout the school year. The teacher would like to encourage Thomas to keep improving his participation and consistency in class.

These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: Informes 3r trimestre Lou-Ellie Colas Acceptable Sam.pdf Kids&Us. She has also reinforced her knowledge of the sizes, the colours, the moods, the types of weather and the genders. She can also count quite well. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lou-Ellie is an easy-going, good-humoured, enthusiastic student. She treats the teacher with respect and care. She generally does so without a problem and follows the teacher's instructions very well. She takes good care of the material and gives it back directly when asked. response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Sam course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Thomas
  • Class: Sam Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas shows moments of attentiveness and understanding, particularly when working with vocabulary like moods, genders, and weather. He enjoys certain activities, such as the "Why are you so sad" story, and can repeat phrases well when engaged. However, his participation is inconsistent—he can be uncooperative at times and requires encouragement to join group routines. While he demonstrates enthusiasm in some lessons, he occasionally resists interaction, such as refusing to high-five the teacher after a reminder about handling materials.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas has a good grasp of foundational vocabulary, including colours, genders, moods, and weather terms. He can repeat weather phrases and perform the corresponding gestures, and he counts well, successfully pairing numbers with spots. However, he struggles with some structures, needing help to recall full phrases or identify animals independently. His knowledge of zoo-related vocabulary and the "Let's go to the zoo" activity is less consistent, as he did not always follow along with gestures like his peers. His comprehension improves with support, and he shows progress in repeating and applying phrases when guided.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Acceptable

Comment:
Thomas is generally cooperative when focused but can be unresponsive or resistant at times. He required physical guidance to join group routines and, along with a classmate, refused to follow a simple social gesture (high-fiving) after a reminder about material care. While he engages well in activities he enjoys, such as story time, his behaviour can be inconsistent—sometimes attentive and other times disengaged. He benefits from clear instructions and reminders to stay on task.

---

Final comment

Thomas has shown flashes of strong comprehension, particularly with core vocabulary like moods, genders, and weather, and he excels in counting and matching tasks. His participation and behaviour, however, remain uneven—he is capable of focus and cooperation but sometimes needs encouragement or redirection. To build consistency, it would be helpful to reinforce routines and positive interactions. Reviewing the audio materials over the break could also support his retention of vocabulary and structures, especially those he finds challenging, like animal names and full phrases. With continued practice, Thomas has the potential to grow more confident and engaged in class.

```

Name: Emma

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Emma is a polite and well-mannered student who generally shows a positive attitude in class. She is calm, respectful, and attentive, and she works well with both her teacher and classmates. At times, she can be a little hesitant to speak up, but with encouragement she is willing to try and becomes more confident. She responds well when given time to think and can be very receptive to guidance. Her kind and cooperative nature contributes positively to the classroom atmosphere.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Emma takes part in class activities and usually completes the work she is asked to do with care. She has shown good effort throughout the term, especially when speaking and working with familiar structures. She can make sentences using the target language and is able to follow the lesson content well. However, she sometimes needs encouragement to try more independently and to avoid relying too much on French. Continuing to build confidence and expanding her vocabulary will help her participate more freely and consistently.

Performance

Emma has made good progress this term. She understands the new vocabulary well and can recognise and use many of the structures covered in class. She is also able to read the stories and chapters well, and her reading is often impressive. In oral activities, she can answer using full structures and usually corrects herself when she makes mistakes, which is very positive. She still needs to work on pronunciation, connectors, and some grammar points such as the third person singular, pronouns, and tense accuracy. With regular practice and more confidence, she will continue to improve.

Marks

She can repeat and use the target language accurately in many situations, and her pronunciation is generally improving. However, she still makes occasional pronunciation mistakes and sometimes needs support to expand her answers.

Writing Mark: 6.80 Good.

Emma's written work shows a good understanding of the language covered this term. She can complete grammar and vocabulary tasks quite well. However, she still makes some errors with capital letters, punctuation, and grammar when writing independently. Further practice with writing and careful revision before tests will help her gain more confidence and accuracy.

Homework mark: 6.50 Acceptable.

Final comment

Over the course of the term, Emma has shown a positive attitude and has made steady progress in class. Her reading skills are particularly strong, and she shows good understanding of the content covered in class. COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huryv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday 2025/2026
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Emma is a polite and well-mannered student who generally shows a positive attitude in class. She is calm, respectful, and attentive, and she works well with both her teacher and classmates. At times, she can be a little hesitant to speak up, but with encouragement she is willing to try and becomes more confident. She responds well when given time to think and can be very receptive to guidance. Her kind and cooperative nature contributes positively to the classroom atmosphere.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Emma takes part in class activities and usually completes the work she is asked to do with care. She has shown good effort throughout the term, especially when speaking and working with familiar structures. She can make sentences using the target language and is able to follow the lesson content well. However, she sometimes needs encouragement to try more independently and to avoid relying too much on French. Continuing to build confidence and expanding her vocabulary will help her participate more freely and consistently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Emma has made good progress this term. She understands the new vocabulary well and can recognise and use many of the structures covered in class. She is also able to read the stories and chapters well, and her reading is often impressive. In oral activities, she can answer using full structures and usually corrects herself when she makes mistakes, which is very positive. She still needs to work on pronunciation, connectors, and some grammar points such as the third person singular, pronouns, and tense accuracy. With regular practice and more confidence, she will continue to improve.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 7.10 Good.

Emma performs well orally when she feels comfortable and has enough time to think. She can repeat and use the target language accurately in many situations, and her pronunciation is generally improving. However, she still makes occasional pronunciation mistakes and sometimes needs support to expand her answers.

Writing Mark: 6.80 Good.

Emma's written work shows a good understanding of the language covered this term. She can complete grammar and vocabulary tasks quite well. However, she still makes some errors with capital letters, punctuation, and grammar when writing independently. Further practice with writing and careful revision before tests will help her gain more confidence and accuracy.

Homework mark: 6.50 Acceptable.

Emma has completed some homework and has generally shown a reasonable effort. To improve further, she should make a more consistent effort to practise at home, revise vocabulary regularly, and review the grammar points covered in class.

Final comment

Over the course of the term, Emma has shown a positive attitude and has made steady progress in class. She is polite, attentive, and increasingly willing to try, especially when she is given a bit of time and support. Her reading skills are particularly strong, and she shows good understanding of the content covered in class. To continue improving, she should focus on speaking more confidently, using English as much as possible, and revising vocabulary and grammar regularly. With continued encouragement and practice, Emma is well placed to make further progress next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Behaviour, Work, Performance) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. All observations must be grounded in the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections (Behaviour/Work/Performance) + Marks + Final comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: constructive and honest, matches examples - balance: weaknesses (pronunciation errors, reliance on French, avoiding speaking) acknowledged alongside strengths

Kids&Us.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: [Not specified in notes]
Level: Fairy Tales 2
Class: Tuesday & Friday

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment: Emma is generally polite, attentive, and motivated in class. She maintains a positive and cooperative attitude, though she occasionally appears uneasy or hesitant. She interacts well with classmates, such as Romain and Ylena, and shows respect when reminded to use English instead of French. Her mood is often good, and she engages in class activities with focus, though she sometimes avoids speaking or requests breaks.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good
Comment: Emma participates in class activities and demonstrates effort in her work. She is serious when completing tasks and shows willingness to improve when given time or encouragement. She sometimes relies on classmates for vocabulary support and occasionally avoids individual tasks, such as writing or speaking, unless prompted. She benefits from collaborative work but may need reminders to try independently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good
Comment: Emma shows a solid understanding of grammar and vocabulary, though she sometimes struggles with pronunciation, tenses (e.g., third-person singular -s), and connectors (e.g., "although," "despite"). She performs well in reading, listening, and comprehension tasks, and can use target language structures effectively when guided. Her writing and speaking contain occasional errors, but she often corrects herself with encouragement. She demonstrates good recall of previous material and can apply it in exercises.

---

Final comment

Emma is a motivated and respectful student who engages well in class activities. She shows strength in reading, comprehension, and using target language structures, though she sometimes hesitates with speaking and writing. With continued encouragement to try independently and practice pronunciation and grammar, she has the potential to improve her confidence and accuracy further.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huruv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment: Emma maintains a polite and attentive attitude in class. She is respectful toward her peers and teacher, though she occasionally asks for vocabulary words in French when lacking the target language. She engages in activities and contributes when prompted, though she sometimes avoids speaking unless directly asked. She reads texts with strong comprehension and pronunciation, though her fluency is developing. She demonstrates improvement in confidence when given more time to respond.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good
Comment: Emma participates actively in class discussions and activities. She completes tasks with seriousness and effort, particularly in grammar and vocabulary exercises. She demonstrates understanding of grammatical structures, including the causative form (*get something done*), comparatives, and connectors (*although, despite*). However, she occasionally struggles with pronunciation, tense consistency (e.g., third-person singular *-s*, *he/she* vs. *do*), and forming sentences with new vocabulary. She benefits from reminders about grammatical rules and sometimes needs encouragement to attempt answers independently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good
Comment: Emma shows solid progress in her language skills. She reads aloud with impressive accuracy, though not always fluently, and summarizes texts using target structures effectively. She understands and applies new vocabulary in context, though she sometimes relies on peers (e.g., Helena, Romain) for translations. Her oral responses improve when given time, and she can correct her own mistakes when prompted. She performs well in listening and writing tasks, though her written work occasionally includes errors in capitalization, punctuation, and sentence structure.

Final comment

Emma has shown steady improvement in her engagement and confidence this term. She is attentive, respectful, and willing to participate, particularly when given structured support. Her reading and comprehension skills are strong, and she actively seeks clarification when needed. While she occasionally avoids speaking due to uncertainty with vocabulary or grammar, she demonstrates resilience by attempting corrections and expanding her answers over time. With continued practice in pronunciation, tense accuracy, and independent sentence-building, she will further solidify her progress. Her collaborative spirit and polite demeanor contribute positively to the classroom environment.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huryv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday 2025/2026
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Emma is a polite and well-mannered student who generally shows a positive attitude in class. She is calm, respectful, and attentive, and she works well with both her teacher and classmates. At times, she can be a little hesitant to speak up, but with encouragement she is willing to try and becomes more confident. She responds well when given time to think and can be very receptive to guidance. Her kind and cooperative nature contributes positively to the classroom atmosphere.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Emma takes part in class activities and usually completes the work she is asked to do with care. She has shown good effort throughout the term, especially when speaking and working with familiar structures. She can make sentences using the target language and is able to follow the lesson content well. However, she sometimes needs encouragement to try more independently and to avoid relying too much on French. Continuing to build confidence and expanding her vocabulary will help her participate more freely and consistently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Emma has made good progress this term. She understands the new vocabulary well and can recognise and use many of the structures covered in class. She is also able to read the stories and chapters well, and her reading is often impressive. In oral activities, she can answer using full structures and usually corrects herself when she makes mistakes, which is very positive. She still needs to work on pronunciation, connectors, and some grammar points such as the third person singular, pronouns, and tense accuracy. With regular practice and more confidence, she will continue to improve.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 7.10 Good.

Emma performs well orally when she feels comfortable and has enough time to think. She can repeat and use the target language accurately in many situations, and her pronunciation is generally improving. However, she still makes occasional pronunciation mistakes and sometimes needs support to expand her answers.

Writing Mark: 6.80 Good.

Emma's written work shows a good understanding of the language covered this term. She can complete grammar and vocabulary tasks quite well. However, she still makes some errors with capital letters, punctuation, and grammar when writing independently. Further practice with writing and careful revision before tests will help her gain more confidence and accuracy.

Homework mark: 6.50 Acceptable.

Emma has completed some homework and has generally shown a reasonable effort. To improve further, she should make a more consistent effort to practise at home, revise vocabulary regularly, and review the grammar points covered in class.

Final comment

Over the course of the term, Emma has shown a positive attitude and has made steady progress in class. She is polite, attentive, and increasingly willing to try, especially when she is given a bit of time and support. Her reading skills are particularly strong, and she shows good understanding of the content covered in class. To continue improving, she should focus on speaking more confidently, using English as much as possible, and revising vocabulary and grammar regularly. With continued encouragement and practice, Emma is well placed to make further progress next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Behaviour, Work, Performance) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. All observations must be grounded in the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections (Behaviour/Work/Performance) + Marks + Final comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: constructive and honest, matches examples - balance: weaknesses (pronunciation errors, reliance on French, avoiding speaking) acknowledged alongside strengths "}]

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huruv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment: Emma maintains a polite and attentive attitude in class. She is respectful toward her peers and teacher, though she occasionally asks for vocabulary words in French when lacking the target language. She engages in activities and contributes when prompted, though she sometimes avoids speaking unless directly asked. She reads texts with strong comprehension and pronunciation, though her fluency is developing. She demonstrates improvement in confidence when given more time to respond.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good
Comment: Emma participates actively in class discussions and activities. She completes tasks with seriousness and effort, particularly in grammar and vocabulary exercises. She demonstrates understanding of grammatical structures, including the causative form (*get something done*), comparatives, and connectors (*although, despite*). However, she occasionally struggles with pronunciation, tense consistency (e.g., third-person singular *-s*, *he/she* vs. *do*), and forming sentences with new vocabulary. She benefits from reminders about grammatical rules and sometimes needs encouragement to attempt answers independently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good
Comment: Emma shows solid progress in her language skills. She reads aloud with impressive accuracy, though not always fluently, and summarizes texts using target structures effectively. She understands and applies new vocabulary in context, though she sometimes relies on peers (e.g., Helena, Romain) for translations. Her oral responses improve when given time, and she can correct her own mistakes when prompted. She performs well in listening and writing tasks, though her written work occasionally includes errors in capitalization, punctuation, and sentence structure.

Final comment

Emma has shown steady improvement in her engagement and confidence this term. She is attentive, respectful, and willing to participate, particularly when given structured support. Her reading and comprehension skills are strong, and she actively seeks clarification when needed. While she occasionally avoids speaking due to uncertainty with vocabulary or grammar, she demonstrates resilience by attempting corrections and expanding her answers over time. With continued practice in pronunciation, tense accuracy, and independent sentence-building, she will further solidify her progress. Her collaborative spirit and polite demeanor contribute positively to the classroom environment.

Behaviour

Comment: Emma is a polite and attentive student who maintains a positive attitude in class. She is respectful toward her classmates and teacher, and her mood is generally good, though she occasionally appears uneasy. She works well collaboratively, particularly with peers like Romain and Helena, and shows increasing confidence when given time to process tasks. Her willingness to ask questions—though sometimes in French—demonstrates her engagement, and she responds well to encouragement to try, even when uncertain.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Comment: Emma participates actively in class activities, particularly when working with others or given structured tasks. She shows a strong grasp of grammar rules, such as the causative form and comparative structures, and can apply them correctly with reminders. However, she occasionally avoids independent tasks, such as writing or speaking exercises, and may seek distractions (e.g., asking to leave the room). Her work improves significantly when she feels supported, as seen in her ability to summarize chapters, answer questions, and use target language from the board. She benefits from peer assistance, especially with vocabulary gaps, and performs best in collaborative settings.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Comment: Emma demonstrates a solid understanding of course material, particularly in reading, grammar exercises, and structured tasks. Her reading skills are impressive, with minimal pronunciation errors when focused, and she can summarize texts and answer questions effectively. However, she struggles with spontaneous speaking, often hesitating or defaulting to French for vocabulary. Her writing shows accuracy in grammar (e.g., "used to" + verb-ing) but requires attention to punctuation and capitalization. Pronunciation and tense consistency—especially third-person singular "-s"—remain areas for improvement. When encouraged, she expands her answers and corrects mistakes, showing potential for greater fluency.

Marks

[No explicit marks provided in notes]

---

Final comment

Emma has made noticeable progress this term, particularly in her reading comprehension, grammar application, and collaborative work. Her polite and attentive demeanor contributes positively to the classroom, and she shows moments of confidence when given time or peer support. To build on her strengths, she should focus on increasing her independence in speaking and writing tasks, reducing reliance on French for vocabulary, and practicing pronunciation and tense consistency. Regular revision of grammar rules (e.g., third-person singular, connectors) and vocabulary will help her feel more secure in spontaneous communication. With continued encouragement and a willingness to take risks, Emma has the potential to grow significantly in her language skills.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huryv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday 2025/2026
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Emma is a polite and well-mannered student who generally shows a positive attitude in class. She is calm, respectful, and attentive, and she works well with both her teacher and classmates. At times, she can be a little hesitant to speak up, but with encouragement she is willing to try and becomes more confident. She responds well when given time to think and can be very receptive to guidance. Her kind and cooperative nature contributes positively to the classroom atmosphere.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Emma takes part in class activities and usually completes the work she is asked to do with care. She has shown good effort throughout the term, especially when speaking and working with familiar structures. She can make sentences using the target language and is able to follow the lesson content well. However, she sometimes needs encouragement to try more independently and to avoid relying too much on French. Continuing to build confidence and expanding her vocabulary will help her participate more freely and consistently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Emma has made good progress this term. She understands the new vocabulary well and can recognise and use many of the structures covered in class. She is also able to read the stories and chapters well, and her reading is often impressive. In oral activities, she can answer using full structures and usually corrects herself when she makes mistakes, which is very positive. She still needs to work on pronunciation, connectors, and some grammar points such as the third person singular, pronouns, and tense accuracy. With regular practice and more confidence, she will continue to improve.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 7.10 Good.

Emma performs well orally when she feels comfortable and has enough time to think. She can repeat and use the target language accurately in many situations, and her pronunciation is generally improving. However, she still makes occasional pronunciation mistakes and sometimes needs support to expand her answers.

Writing Mark: 6.80 Good.

Emma's written work shows a good understanding of the language covered this term. She can complete grammar and vocabulary tasks quite well. However, she still makes some errors with capital letters, punctuation, and grammar when writing independently. Further practice with writing and careful revision before tests will help her gain more confidence and accuracy.

Homework mark: 6.50 Acceptable.

Emma has completed some homework and has generally shown a reasonable effort. To improve further, she should make a more consistent effort to practise at home, revise vocabulary regularly, and review the grammar points covered in class.

Final comment

Over the course of the term, Emma has shown a positive attitude and has made steady progress in class. Kids&Us.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Emma
Teacher: Huryv Osmanova
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday 2025/2026
Absences: 0

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Emma is a polite and well-mannered student who generally shows a positive attitude in class. She is calm, respectful, and attentive, and she works well with both her teacher and classmates. At times, she can be a little hesitant to speak up, but with encouragement she is willing to try and becomes more confident. She responds well when given time to think and can be very receptive to guidance. Her kind and cooperative nature contributes positively to the classroom atmosphere.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Emma takes part in class activities and usually completes the work she is asked to do with care. She has shown good effort throughout the term, especially when speaking and working with familiar structures. She can make sentences using the target language and is able to follow the lesson content well. However, she sometimes needs encouragement to try more independently and to avoid relying too much on French. Continuing to build confidence and expanding her vocabulary will help her participate more freely and consistently.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Emma has made good progress this term. She understands the new vocabulary well and can recognise and use many of the structures covered in class. She is also able to read the stories and chapters well, and her reading is often impressive. In oral activities, she can answer using full structures and usually corrects herself when she makes mistakes, which is very positive. She still needs to work on pronunciation, connectors, and some grammar points such as the third person singular, pronouns, and tense accuracy. With regular practice and more confidence, she will continue to improve.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 7.10 Good.

Emma performs well orally when she feels comfortable and has enough time to think. She can repeat and use the target language accurately in many situations, and her pronunciation is generally improving. However, she still makes occasional pronunciation mistakes and sometimes needs support to expand her answers.

Writing Mark: 6.80 Good.

Emma's written work shows a good understanding of the language covered this term. She can complete grammar and vocabulary tasks quite well. However, she still makes some errors with capital letters, punctuation, and grammar when writing independently. Further practice with writing and careful revision before tests will help her gain more confidence and accuracy.

Homework mark: 6.50 Acceptable.

Emma has completed some homework and has generally shown a reasonable effort. To improve further, she should make a more consistent effort to practise at home, revise vocabulary regularly, and review the grammar points covered in class.

Final comment

Over the course of the term, Emma has shown a positive attitude and has made steady progress in class. She is polite, attentive, and increasingly willing to try, especially when she is given a bit of time and support. Her reading skills are particularly strong, and she shows good understanding of the content covered in class. To continue improving, she should focus on speaking more confidently, using English as much as possible, and revising vocabulary and grammar regularly. With continued encouragement and practice, Emma is well placed to make further progress next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Behaviour, Work, Performance) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. All observations must be grounded in the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections (Behaviour/Work/Performance) + Marks + Final comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: constructive and honest, matches examples - balance: weaknesses (pronunciation errors, reliance on French, avoiding speaking) acknowledged alongside strengths

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Emma
  • Teacher: [Teacher's name not provided in notes]
  • Class: Fairy Tales 2 (Tuesday & Friday)
  • Absences: [Not explicitly mentioned in notes]

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment: Emma is a polite and attentive student who maintains a positive attitude in class. She is respectful toward her classmates and teacher, and her mood is generally good, though she occasionally appears uneasy. She works well collaboratively, particularly with peers like Romain and Helena, and shows increasing confidence when given time to process tasks. Her willingness to ask questions—though sometimes in French—demonstrates her engagement, and she responds well to encouragement to try, even when uncertain.

Work

Assessment (Work): Good

Comment: Emma participates actively in class activities, particularly when working with others or given structured tasks. She shows a strong grasp of grammar rules, such as the causative form and comparative structures, and can apply them correctly with reminders. However, she occasionally avoids independent tasks, such as writing or speaking exercises, and may seek distractions (e.g., asking to leave the room). Her work improves significantly when she feels supported, as seen in her ability to summarize chapters, answer questions, and use target language from the board. She benefits from peer assistance, especially with vocabulary gaps, and performs best in collaborative settings.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Comment: Emma demonstrates a solid understanding of course material, particularly in reading, grammar exercises, and structured tasks. Her reading skills are impressive, with minimal pronunciation errors when focused, and she can summarize texts and answer questions effectively. However, she struggles with spontaneous speaking, often hesitating or defaulting to French for vocabulary. Her writing shows accuracy in grammar (e.g., "used to" + verb-ing) but requires attention to punctuation and capitalization. Pronunciation and tense consistency—especially third-person singular "-s"—remain areas for improvement. When encouraged, she expands her answers and corrects mistakes, showing potential for greater fluency.

Marks

[No explicit marks provided in notes]

---

Final comment

Emma has made noticeable progress this term, particularly in her reading comprehension, grammar application, and collaborative work. Her polite and attentive demeanor contributes positively to the classroom, and she shows moments of confidence when given time or peer support. To build on her strengths, she should focus on increasing her independence in speaking and writing tasks, reducing reliance on French for vocabulary, and practicing pronunciation and tense consistency. Regular revision of grammar rules (e.g., third-person singular, connectors) and vocabulary will help her feel more secure in spontaneous communication. With continued encouragement and a willingness to take risks, Emma has the potential to grow significantly in her language skills.

```

- tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

componentResults.reason: The report mostly follows the required Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour, and Final comment structure, with assessment ratings and comments for the three main sections. It synthesises multiple notes into a cohesive, warm narrative. However, it adds an extra 'Marks' section not supported by the required structure and not grounded in any actual mark or test data. There are also several statements that are only weakly traceable or somewhat invented, such as Chloé sharing materials, treating peers and teacher with respect, seeking extra attention, doing better because the class is smaller/less overwhelming, and recommendations involving audio tracks. Overall it is a good report but with some grounding and structure issues.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a playful and energetic mood, though she can sometimes be distracted or tired. She shows willingness to participate in activities, especially those involving storytelling, age and gender identification, and interactive games like "What's missing?" and dressing Emma. Chloé enjoys hands-on tasks, such as placing clothes items and stickers on body parts, and she engages well with her classmates, particularly when working in smaller groups. However, she occasionally holds back when new material is introduced, requiring reminders or assistance to fully participate. For example, she repeatedly asked for clarification on tasks she already understood, which affected her focus. Once redirected, she cooperated well with both her teacher and peers.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though she still faces challenges with certain vocabulary and structures. She can identify and use basic concepts like clean/dirty and dry/wet, and she understands simple instructions for activities such as dressing Emma or placing tattoos on body parts. Chloé can tell her age and gender using nearly complete structures and has shown improvement in pairing actions with rooms (e.g., associating activities with their correct locations). However, she struggles with colours and clothing vocabulary, often requiring teacher support to recall or apply these terms. Her comprehension of the story improved over time, though she initially needed significant help to retell it. Chloé performs well in worksheet activities and counting tasks, demonstrating strength in structured, repetitive exercises. To progress, she should focus on internalising vocabulary—particularly colours and clothing—and practising full sentences independently.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and sociable student who generally integrates well with her classmates. She responds positively to reminders about classroom behaviour, such as the traffic light system, which helps her refocus when the group becomes loud or playful. While she can be easily distracted—especially when tired or when instructions are given—she usually listens after a gentle prompt. Chloé treats her peers and teacher with respect, though she occasionally tests boundaries by asking repetitive questions or seeking extra attention. She shares materials and participates in group activities, particularly when the class is smaller and less overwhelming. Her mood and focus improve noticeably in a calm, structured environment.

Marks

Comment:
Chloé’s performance reflects her growing familiarity with the course content, though her results vary depending on her focus and the complexity of the task. She excels in activities that involve repetition, such as counting or worksheet exercises, and shows enthusiasm for storytelling once she grasps the narrative. However, her inconsistent attention—especially with new vocabulary—limits her ability to use structures independently. With continued practice, particularly in listening and recalling terms like colours and clothing, Chloé’s confidence and accuracy will improve. Keep up the effort, Chloé!

---

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her willingness to engage with activities and follow instructions. While she initially struggled with key vocabulary and story comprehension, her ability to apply structures—such as describing Emma’s clothes or identifying body parts—has strengthened. Her behaviour has also improved; she responds well to redirection and collaborates nicely with her classmates. To build on this progress, it would be beneficial for Chloé to review vocabulary regularly, especially through audio tracks or interactive games, to reinforce her memory and comprehension. With her playful and curious nature, she has the potential to thrive with consistent practice and a bit more focus. Well done, Chloé—keep up the good work!

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples "}]

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a playful and energetic mood, though she can sometimes be distracted or tired. She shows willingness to participate in activities, especially those involving storytelling, age and gender identification, and interactive games like "What's missing?" and dressing Emma. Chloé enjoys hands-on tasks, such as placing clothes items and stickers on body parts, and she engages well with her classmates, particularly when working in smaller groups. However, she occasionally holds back when new material is introduced, requiring reminders or assistance to fully participate. For example, she repeatedly asked for clarification on tasks she already understood, which affected her focus. Once redirected, she cooperated well with both her teacher and peers.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though she still faces challenges with certain vocabulary and structures. She can identify and use basic concepts like clean/dirty and dry/wet, and she understands simple instructions for activities such as dressing Emma or placing tattoos on body parts. Chloé can tell her age and gender using nearly complete structures and has shown improvement in pairing actions with rooms (e.g., associating activities with their correct locations). However, she struggles with colours and clothing vocabulary, often requiring teacher support to recall or apply these terms. Her comprehension of the story improved over time, though she initially needed significant help to retell it. Chloé performs well in worksheet activities and counting tasks, demonstrating strength in structured, repetitive exercises. To progress, she should focus on internalising vocabulary—particularly colours and clothing—and practising full sentences independently.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and sociable student who generally integrates well with her classmates. She responds positively to reminders about classroom behaviour, such as the traffic light system, which helps her refocus when the group becomes loud or playful. While she can be easily distracted—especially when tired or when instructions are given—she usually listens after a gentle prompt. Chloé treats her peers and teacher with respect, though she occasionally tests boundaries by asking repetitive questions or seeking extra attention. She shares materials and participates in group activities, particularly when the class is smaller and less overwhelming. Her mood and focus improve noticeably in a calm, structured environment.

Marks

Comment:
Chloé’s performance reflects her growing familiarity with the course content, though her results vary depending on her focus and the complexity of the task. She excels in activities that involve repetition, such as counting or worksheet exercises, and shows enthusiasm for storytelling once she grasps the narrative. However, her inconsistent attention—especially with new vocabulary—limits her ability to use structures independently. With continued practice, particularly in listening and recalling terms like colours and clothing, Chloé’s confidence and accuracy will improve. Keep up the effort, Chloé!

---

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her willingness to engage with activities and follow instructions. While she initially struggled with key vocabulary and story comprehension, her ability to apply structures—such as describing Emma’s clothes or identifying body parts—has strengthened. Her behaviour has also improved; she responds well to redirection and collaborates nicely with her classmates. To build on this progress, it would be beneficial for Chloé to review vocabulary regularly, especially through audio tracks or interactive games, to reinforce her memory and comprehension. With her playful and curious nature, she has the potential to thrive with consistent practice and a bit more focus. Well done, Chloé—keep up the good work!

```

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples "}]

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples
