[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: Informes 3r trimestre Camille Martin null.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Camille Martin Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 2 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Camille comes to class in a good mood and a smile on her face. She participates enthusiastically in all the activities. Camille truly loves story time. She likes reciting the story with her classmates and her teacher. Camille also really enjoys the "Numbers song", the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game" and the "Where is Emma's tattoo?" activities. Camille loves singing along to the songs and she also shows a real interest in the worksheet activities. Camille is always cooperative with her teacher and her classmates. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Camille's English knowledge is good. She knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. She can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, she specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). She has also reinforced her knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Camille is a joyful, attentive, friendly and bright student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with respect and care. She listens attentively in class and usually stays focused during the lesson, seldom being distracted by her classmates. On a few occasions, she jokingly kept the material instead of giving it back to the teacher, but usually, gives it back directly when asked. Moreover, she takes good care of the material and always shares it with her classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 7.20 Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. The decrease in her audio listening frequency might explain her good result, instead of a very good one! The teacher still wants to congratulate Camille on her good result! Keep up the good work! --- Final comment At the beginning of the school year, Camille was a very quiet and shy student. Thanks to her classmates’ friendliness and her easy-going attitude, she came out of her shell and has felt comfortable in the class ever since. It has truly been a joy to have Camille in class, thanks to her smiling and her good-humoured attitude. Furthermore, she has also made progress in her English learning, developing both her memory and her comprehension skills. It is recommended to listen to the audio tracks during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures, learnt throughout the school year. ### Example 2: Informes 3r trimestre Luis Moreau Very Good Emma.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Luis Moreau Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 0 Frequency of use (audio): Pass Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Luis usually comes to class in a good and calm mood. He usually participates in the activities with a certain enthusiasm. Luis really likes story time and he takes an active role in the storytelling, reciting the story with the teacher. He also seemed to really enjoy the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. Luis is always cooperative with his classmates and his teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Very good Comment: Luis' English knowledge is very good. He knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. He can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, he specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g. sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). He has also reinforced his knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Luis is a composed, gentle, friendly and attentive student. He always treats his classmates and his teacher with respect and care. He is seldom distracted by his classmates or external elements. He usually stays focused and listens attentively, following the teacher's instructions very well. Luis, always treats the material with care, gives the material back directly when asked, and shares it with his classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.40 Very good. Luis can understand the question and makes an effort to use a complete structure in the answer, even if it is not always the correct one. Congratulations on your very good result, Luis! We're very proud of you! --- Final comment Throughout the year, Luis has opened up and has seemed to really feel more comfortable with his classmates. It is a real joy to have Luis in class, thanks to his diligent and mindful attitude, as well as his friendliness. Moreover, Luis kept developing both his memory and comprehension skills. His oral test results have been in constant progress. Well done, Luis! Furthermore, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the school year, it is recommended to listen to the audio track during the summer holidays. ### Example 3: Informes 3r trimestre Rayan Bernard de Fortemps Acceptable Emma.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Rayan Bernard Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 0 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Rayan usually comes to class in a cheerful and lively mood. He participates in the activities with great enthusiasm. Rayan does not seem overly interested in story time, usually losing focus during that activity. However, he really enjoyed the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where is Emma's tattoo?", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. Rayan is usually quite cooperative with his classmates and his teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory Comment: Rayan's English knowledge is satisfactory. He does know a certain amount of vocabulary and structures. However, he usually requires the teacher's help to repeat the full structures and tell him how he must use words in sentences. This term he specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). He has also reinforced his knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Good Comment: Rayan is a fun-loving, energetic and good-humoured student. He seldom treats his classmates in an unfriendly manner, generally treating them with respect and care. He also generally treats his teacher in a respectful manner. Rayan can still, at times, be distracted by external elements and his classmates. However, he has learnt to stay more focused and listen more attentively to the teacher, following the teacher's instructions rather well. He tends to treat the material with care and can give it back directly when asked, and usually shares it with his classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 6.20 Acceptable. Rayan can understand the question but, in most cases, still does not respond with a complete structure. The teacher had noticed a real improvement in Rayan's learning, when Rayan's audio listening frequency was more regular. He was able to understand what was expected of him during the activities and the meaning of the words used in the activities. Keep up the good work, Rayan! --- Final comment Throughout the school year and especially this term, Rayan’s behaviour has really improved. He still needs to move around and explore his surroundings, but he does so in a calmer way. He has been getting better at listening to the teacher and staying more focused. He has kept his grades up and with some work and a regular audio listening, his English level can only increase! It is recommended to listen to the audio tracks during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the year. ## Important Additional Instructions from User - Takes precedence over any provided example MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the sections ## Student Notes (source of truth — all report content must derive from these) Student: Chloé, Class: Emma Saturday - 2026-05-14: Chloé did not know the colours or the clothes. The Emma Saturday students were fun. They behaved quite well and sometimes needed to be reminded that they could be in orange instead of green, and it worked wonders. Léo knew or at least understood the worksheet activity very well. They all could understand the clean and dirty concept and dry and wet too. They could all repeat the structures well and understood the Emma is wearing… activity. - 2026-05-14: They all behaved quite well and sometimes needed to be reminded that they could be in orange instead of green, and it worked wonders. Chloé did not know the colours or the clothes. Léo knew or at least understood the worksheet activity very well. They could all understand the clean and dirty concept, dry and wet too. They could all repeat the structures well and understood the "Emma is wearing..." activity. - 2026-05-14: They were all a bit loud to begin with, so I needed to put in place the traffic light, and after that they participated super well. They all could tell their age and their gender. They could all repeat the story for story time and seemed to know a few words. They all could put the clothes items on and take them off, and with Théo’s help, Léo, Noah and Chloé could put the correct coloured items. Chloé kept asking over and over again where she needed to place the things when she actually knew the response and it was a tad annoying. She stopped when we told her to because it even annoyed Léo. They could all do the worksheet activity very well and could count super well. They could ask for the counters and could understand the corresponding colour and the clothing item on their bingo card. Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: [Not specified in notes]
Level: Emma Saturday
Absences: [Not specified in notes]

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good
Comment:

Chloé participates in class activities with enthusiasm, though her level of engagement varies depending on the task. She enjoys story time and can repeat the story with help, demonstrating a willingness to learn. Chloé actively takes part in worksheet activities and games, such as pairing body parts with clean/dirty concepts and placing tattoos on the correct body parts. She cooperates with her classmates and teacher, though she sometimes needs reminders to stay focused. Chloé asks repetitive questions when instructions are given, which can disrupt the flow of the lesson but improves when corrected. She shows excitement for new activities, such as the new story about Emma’s tattoo, and engages well when the group is calm.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory
Comment:

Chloé’s English knowledge is developing. She can use some structures and vocabulary but often requires reminders or help to complete sentences correctly. She knows her age and gender, can pair body parts with clean/dirty concepts, and understands basic instructions like dressing and undressing Emma with the correct clothing items. Chloé can repeat new vocabulary and structures but sometimes struggles with recalling the full phrases independently. She performs well in worksheet activities and counting tasks but needs reinforcement in mastering clothing vocabulary and story narration. Her counting skills and ability to follow worksheet instructions are strong points.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment:

Chloé is generally well-behaved and respects her classmates and teacher. She follows instructions when reminded and participates in group activities. However, she can be easily distracted, especially when the group is loud or when she is tired. Chloé sometimes interrupts with repetitive questions but responds well to corrections. She treats classroom materials with care and engages positively in activities like the traffic light system to improve focus. Chloé’s mood is generally good, and she contributes to a positive classroom environment.

Final comment

Chloé has shown steady progress this term, particularly in her willingness to participate and her ability to follow instructions. She enjoys new activities and engages well with her peers, though she sometimes needs reminders to stay focused. Her counting skills and worksheet performance are strong, and she demonstrates enthusiasm for learning. With continued practice and reinforcement of vocabulary and structures, Chloé’s English skills will continue to develop. It is recommended to reinforce listening and repetition of audio tracks to support her learning over the summer.

componentResults.reason: The report follows the required structure with Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour, and a Final comment, and each main section includes an assessment and comment. It synthesises multiple notes into a cohesive narrative and maintains a generally warm, encouraging tone. Most statements are traceable to the source notes, including Chloé’s difficulty with colours/clothes, understanding of clean/dirty and dry/wet, ability to tell age/gender, need for reminders, distraction/tiredness, repeated 'quoi quoi quoi' behaviour, and enjoyment of some activities. However, a few claims are somewhat inferred or not directly supported, such as saying she 'holds back when new material is introduced,' that she 'treats her classmates and teacher respectfully,' and that her comprehension of the Emma-is-wearing activity is good while relying on prompts. The Data section avoids invented absences or marks, which is good. Overall, it is a solid report with minor grounding issues.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples "}]

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: [Not specified in notes]
Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Chloé usually participates in class activities, though her engagement varies. She shows enthusiasm for certain tasks, such as the "What's missing" activity and worksheet exercises, which she enjoys and completes well. However, she sometimes holds back when new material is introduced, requiring additional support to fully engage. Chloé is generally cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she occasionally needs reminders to focus.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is developing but remains inconsistent. She can tell her age and gender using the correct structures and understands concepts like clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can repeat new vocabulary and structures, and with guidance, she can pair actions with the correct rooms. However, she struggles with colours and clothing vocabulary, often requiring teacher assistance to recall or apply these. Chloé can follow along with story time and workshop activities but needs reminders to use full structures independently. Her comprehension of the "Emma is wearing..." activity is good, though she relies on prompts.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a playful and generally well-behaved student. She integrates well within the group, though she can be distracted or tired, particularly when instructions are given. On some occasions, she repeatedly asks for clarification (e.g., "quoi quoi quoi") even when she understands, which can disrupt the flow of activities. However, she responds well to reminders and stops when asked. Chloé treats her classmates and teacher respectfully and follows the group's mood, calming down when the class is reminded to do so.

---

Final comment

Chloé has shown progress in her ability to follow instructions and participate in group activities. While she sometimes struggles with focus and new vocabulary, she demonstrates a willingness to engage and improves when given clear guidance. Her understanding of basic structures and concepts is solid, but she would benefit from additional practice with colours and clothing terms. With continued effort and attention to teacher instructions, Chloé can further develop her confidence and independence in using English. It is recommended to reinforce vocabulary at home to support her learning.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: [Teacher's name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:

Mila shows a generally positive attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her interests. She eagerly participates in hands-on tasks like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, where she demonstrates enthusiasm and a willingness to engage. Her motivation is strongest when she feels confident in the material, such as when retelling stories or using familiar vocabulary and structures. However, Mila tends to withdraw or resist participation when she doesn’t get her way—whether it’s not receiving the blu-tack, missing out on the stamp activity, or not being able to see the Big Book. These moments occasionally lead to frustration, where she may lay down, turn away, or refuse to join activities like the True or False game. Encouragingly, her mood often improves with gentle redirection, and she reconnects with the group, especially in collaborative tasks like handing out materials.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has demonstrated a solid grasp of the course content, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She knows most of the structures, songs, and gestures tied to the Oliver themes and can repeat them accurately. Her strengths lie in combining moods and actions, using weather and clothing vocabulary (e.g., pairing phrases with weather cards), and describing story elements with minimal support. She excels in activities like the worksheet tasks and color vocabulary exercises, where she confidently applies what she’s learned. However, Mila occasionally struggles with newer or more abstract concepts, such as the words "taking off" and "putting on" during weather routines. These areas require additional practice to build fluency. Her ability to retell stories almost independently highlights her progress, though her participation fluctuates when faced with challenges or unmet expectations.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:

Mila’s behaviour in class is generally cooperative, but her engagement is closely tied to her emotional state. She integrates well with the group during enjoyable activities, such as games and storytelling, where she contributes positively and follows instructions. However, she can become easily frustrated when things don’t go her way—whether it’s not winning a game, not receiving a material she wants, or feeling overlooked (e.g., not seeing the Big Book). These moments may lead to her laying down, turning away, or briefly disengaging. While she responds to reminders to refocus, her mood sometimes affects her ability to stay on task, particularly toward the end of longer activities when the class as a whole grows restless. Mila’s interactions with peers are friendly, and she shows willingness to help (e.g., distributing materials), but her patience and resilience in frustrating situations remain areas for growth.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown notable progress this term, particularly in her vocabulary acquisition, story retelling, and participation in group activities. Her enthusiasm for games and hands-on tasks is a strength, and she applies her knowledge confidently when motivated. To build on this foundation, it will be important for Mila to practise managing frustration when faced with challenges or unmet expectations, as this will help her stay engaged even in less preferred activities. Continued reinforcement of newer vocabulary, such as action words during routines, will further solidify her skills. With her curiosity and ability to bounce back after setbacks, Mila has the potential to thrive with consistent encouragement and opportunities to take on leadership roles in class.

Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: [Teacher's name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:

Mila shows a generally positive attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her interests. She eagerly participates in hands-on tasks like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, where she demonstrates enthusiasm and a willingness to engage. Her motivation is strongest when she feels confident in the material, such as when retelling stories or using familiar vocabulary and structures. However, Mila tends to withdraw or resist participation when she doesn’t get her way—whether it’s not receiving the blu-tack, missing out on the stamp activity, or not being able to see the Big Book. These moments occasionally lead to frustration, where she may lay down, turn away, or refuse to join activities like the True or False game. Encouragingly, her mood often improves with gentle redirection, and she reconnects with the group, especially in collaborative tasks like handing out materials.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has demonstrated a solid grasp of the course content, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She knows most of the structures, songs, and gestures tied to the Oliver themes and can repeat them accurately. Her strengths lie in combining moods and actions, using weather and clothing vocabulary (e.g., pairing phrases with weather cards), and describing story elements with minimal support. She excels in activities like the worksheet tasks and color vocabulary exercises, where she confidently applies what she’s learned. However, Mila occasionally struggles with newer or more abstract concepts, such as the words "taking off" and "putting on" during weather routines. These areas require additional practice to build fluency. Her ability to retell stories almost independently highlights her progress, though her participation fluctuates when faced with challenges or unmet expectations.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:

Mila’s behaviour in class is generally cooperative, but her engagement is closely tied to her emotional state. She integrates well with the group during enjoyable activities, such as games and storytelling, where she contributes positively and follows instructions. However, she can become easily frustrated when things don’t go her way—whether it’s not winning a game, not receiving a material she wants, or feeling overlooked (e.g., not seeing the Big Book). These moments may lead to her laying down, turning away, or briefly disengaging. While she responds to reminders to refocus, her mood sometimes affects her ability to stay on task, particularly toward the end of longer activities when the class as a whole grows restless. Mila’s interactions with peers are friendly, and she shows willingness to help (e.g., distributing materials), but her patience and resilience in frustrating situations remain areas for growth.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown notable progress this term, particularly in her vocabulary acquisition, story retelling, and participation in group activities. Her enthusiasm for games and hands-on tasks is a strength, and she applies her knowledge confidently when motivated. To build on this foundation, it will be important for Mila to practise managing frustration when faced with challenges or unmet expectations, as this will help her stay engaged even in less preferred activities. Continued reinforcement of newer vocabulary, such as action words during routines, will further solidify her skills. With her curiosity and ability to bounce back after setbacks, Mila has the potential to thrive with consistent encouragement and opportunities to take on leadership roles in class.

```

results.error: The report is generally well grounded in the notes, maintains an encouraging but honest tone, and constructively addresses Mila’s frustration and withdrawal. However, it does not fully meet the required structure because it omits the Marks section and Oral Test Mark entirely. ---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila
Teacher: (Not specified)
Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Mila generally shows a happy and willing attitude in class, particularly when engaging in activities she enjoys, such as action tasks, songs, and games like Family Bingo and the Dice Game. She demonstrates a strong grasp of vocabulary, structures, and story content, often participating eagerly. However, her motivation fluctuates when she does not get her way, such as during the stamp activity or when denied the blu-tack, leading her to momentarily withdraw from participation. She also holds back slightly when new material is introduced but regains confidence once she internalizes it.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Very good

Comment:
Mila has shown excellent progress in vocabulary acquisition, story retelling, and understanding structures. She can combine moods and actions effectively, use weather and clothing vocabulary accurately, and recall story details with minimal assistance. Her ability to pair sentences with realia items and complete worksheet activities demonstrates her strong comprehension. However, she occasionally needs support with specific terms like "taking off" and "putting on" during routines. Continued practice with these areas will help solidify her skills.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally well-integrated into the group and contributes positively to activities. However, she sometimes struggles with emotional regulation, such as when she could not see the Big Book or was denied the blu-tack, leading her to lay down, turn away, or refuse to participate temporarily. She also tends to close up when things do not go her way but usually recovers and re-engages. Her focus wanes toward the end of longer activities, as seen during story time, but she remains cooperative and enthusiastic in most group tasks.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has made notable strides in her language learning, demonstrating strong vocabulary retention and story comprehension. While her participation is generally high, she sometimes needs encouragement to stay engaged when faced with challenges or unmet expectations. With continued practice and emotional support, she will further develop her confidence and consistency in class. Well done, Mila!

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report. Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence. IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples — only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes. ### Example 1: pdfAvaluacioRo.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Romane Leclerc Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Romane displays a keen enthusiasm for learning, particularly evident during vocabulary sessions. She consistently engages with activities, showcasing a willingness to assist in storytelling. Her high level of interest portrays a positive attitude, inspiring her classmates as they work together on different tasks and games. Romane's commitment to participating in exercises, even during challenging activities like singing and miming, reflects her motivation to succeed. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Romane has made commendable progress in her vocabulary acquisition, particularly when discussing Oliver and his family members. She actively engages in activities such as story retelling and dice games, enhancing her understanding of the material. However, Romane has faced challenges with certain grammar concepts, including prepositions. It is essential for her to continue practising these areas to further expand her language skills and confidence in speaking. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Romane exhibits positive behaviour throughout class, interacting well with her peers and contributing to a collaborative environment. Her involvement in group activities encourages a supportive atmosphere. She shows respect to both classmates and the teacher and expresses encouragement towards others, supporting a healthy learning environment while still developing patience in group settings. Romane's respectful conduct plays an essential role in maintaining the class's focus. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.00 Good. Romane can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. --- Final comment ________________________________________________________________________________ Romane has had a successful year, showing significant growth in her English language skills. Her motivation and engagement in class activities are commendable, and she has gradually acquired vocabulary and grammar structures. Continued practice with audio materials over the summer will undoubtedly support her ongoing learning journey. I look forward to seeing her further develop her skills in the coming year. ### Example 2: pdfAvaluacioCl.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Clémence Legrand Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 3 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory Comment: Clémence shows an acceptable level of motivation and enthusiasm during class activities, particularly during engaging games like the dice game. Although she encounters challenges with vocabulary, her willingness to participate is commendable. She attempts to interact with lesson materials and her peers despite some struggles. Encouragement to keep engaging positively will help her continue to grow and develop her confidence in classroom interactions. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Poor Comment: Clémence is currently facing challenges with vocabulary, particularly words like young and old, as well as with constructing complete sentences. While she has shown understanding during specific activities, such as identifying clothing for different weather conditions, consistent attention to vocabulary is essential for her progress. To support her learning, it is recommended that Clémence continues to revisit vocabulary at home, helping her to build upon her knowledge and confidence in class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Good Comment: Clémence is generally a pleasant student but occasionally struggles to focus and stay on task. She sometimes needs reminders to pay attention and limit conversations with her friends during lessons. Despite these distractions, she listens well when prompted. Her behaviour towards group activities is friendly, and she interacts positively with her classmates. By fostering her ability to maintain focus, Clémence can further enhance her learning and classroom experience. Marks Oral Test Mark: 5.90 Needs to improve. Clémence shows a good understanding of the question, but needs to hear the sound of the first word from the teacher to provide an answer and uses only one word when answering. --- Final comment ____________________________________________________________ Clémence has encountered several difficulties this year, particularly with vocabulary retention and attention during class activities. While she displays enthusiasm in group settings, her distractibility hinders her progress. Moving forward, it's crucial that she continues to practise her vocabulary over the summer. With dedicated effort, she has the potential to improve her learning significantly. ### Example 3: pdfAvaluacioLyam.pdf Kids&Us. Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Lyam Renard Meli Teacher: Thérèse Garnier Level: Oliver 2024/2025 Absences: 1 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Excellent Comment: Lyam continues to radiate enthusiasm in class, contributing positively to the learning environment. He eagerly participates in all activities, demonstrating a notable improvement in waiting his turn to speak, even when keen to share. His comfort within the group fosters motivation, making him enjoy lessons more. Lyam approaches every task with readiness and a positive mindset, ensuring his engagement levels remain high and inspiring his peers. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Excellent Comment: Lyam's learning has been exemplary this year. He excels at retelling stories and has shown noticeable improvement in making complete sentences. His ability to describe characteristics of Oliver's family members, indicates his understanding of the material. He quickly identifies and corrects his mistakes, showcasing his awareness and dedication to learning. Overall, his level of engagement is excellent, and he enjoys demonstrating what he has learned during class. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Lyam exhibits excellent behaviour in the classroom, showing respect for his teachers and kindness towards classmates. He enjoys participating in activities, often assisting others when he can. His eagerness to take part is commendable, though he needs occasional reminders to wait for his turn to speak. Overall, his cooperative approach contributes positively to group dynamics and the classroom environment. Marks Oral Test Mark: 9.90 Excellent. Lyam can understand the question and, in most cases, answers using a structure which is complete and accurate. Excellent work! --- Final comment ____________________________________________ Lyam has had a wonderfully productive year, showcasing outstanding learning and behavioural qualities. He has made significant strides in both his participation and his ability to articulate thoughts in complete sentences. Well done, Lyam, and keep up this exceptional journey in learning. ## Important Additional Instructions from User - Takes precedence over any provided example MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone ## Student Notes (source of truth — all report content must derive from these) Student: Mila, Class: Oliver Thursday - 2026-05-21: Mila came to class in a very happy mood. She knew some vocabulary and could repeat the structures too. She needed help with the "taking off" and "putting on" words during the weather routines. She got upset because she could not see the Big Book during story time, but did not move to be able to see. She laid down a few times, was asked to sit, and turned her back to me during the whole story because she was angry. She could do the action activity quite well and was happy to participate. - 2026-05-14: The Oliver Thursday students were all great and participated in the activities very well. Mila did close up a bit when she did not get her way, but it got better. They all loved the present activity, all wanted to help hand out the material, and they all got a green face. - 2026-05-14: Mila only listened at first. She was a bit upset because she couldn’t do the stamp and then did not take part in the routines. She then said she had a headache and did take part in the activities. The class could tell who was missing and name the family member, repeat the correct structure each time, pair up the correct sentence with the weather realia item, and say most of the words, although they sometimes needed a bit of help. They needed to move a lot and seemed to get bored by the end of the story. - 2026-05-14: Mila could tell the story with almost no help. She got upset by the end of class because I did not allow her to keep the blu-tack. She refused to take part in the True or False activity and the Dice Game. - 2026-05-14: Mila came today. She knew most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures, and story. They all loved A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. - 2026-05-14: Mila could combine the mood and the actions very well. She knew the phrase that went with her weather card, and she knew the story quite well. She could already tell part of it and repeat the rest. They learned how to say what they were wearing, and they learned the color white and the color black today. They could do the weather and clothing item combination very well, and they did the worksheet activity very, very well. They also showed that they knew the color vocabulary, which was great. All of them could do the color vocabulary, and I was really proud of them, and their behavior was relatively great today, so I was really happy with them. - 2026-05-14: Mila already knew the structures and understood how to use them, so she could repeat them very well. She could also use the new vocabulary words and understood how to use them during the activities. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. Output the report as clean HTML (using

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila
Teacher: (Not specified)
Level: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Mila generally shows a happy and willing attitude in class, particularly when engaging in activities she enjoys, such as action tasks, songs, and games like Family Bingo and the Dice Game. She demonstrates a strong grasp of vocabulary, structures, and story content, often participating eagerly. However, her motivation fluctuates when she does not get her way, such as during the stamp activity or when denied the blu-tack, leading her to momentarily withdraw from participation. She also holds back slightly when new material is introduced but regains confidence once she internalizes it.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Very good

Comment:
Mila has shown excellent progress in vocabulary acquisition, story retelling, and understanding structures. She can combine moods and actions effectively, use weather and clothing vocabulary accurately, and recall story details with minimal assistance. Her ability to pair sentences with realia items and complete worksheet activities demonstrates her strong comprehension. However, she occasionally needs support with specific terms like "taking off" and "putting on" during routines. Continued practice with these areas will help solidify her skills.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally well-integrated into the group and contributes positively to activities. However, she sometimes struggles with emotional regulation, such as when she could not see the Big Book or was denied the blu-tack, leading her to lay down, turn away, or refuse to participate temporarily. She also tends to close up when things do not go her way but usually recovers and re-engages. Her focus wanes toward the end of longer activities, as seen during story time, but she remains cooperative and enthusiastic in most group tasks.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has made notable strides in her language learning, demonstrating strong vocabulary retention and story comprehension. While her participation is generally high, she sometimes needs encouragement to stay engaged when faced with challenges or unmet expectations. With continued practice and emotional support, she will further develop her confidence and consistency in class. Well done, Mila!

```

Well done,\nLyam, and keep up this exceptional journey in learning." } ] Instructions given to the model: MOTIVATION AND PARTICIPATION: o Motivation: definition: factor or group of factors that bring about a specific type of behaviour. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Mila
Teacher: [Teacher Name]
Level: Oliver 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:

Mila demonstrates a positive attitude toward classroom activities, arriving in a happy mood and showing willingness to participate. She actively engages in tasks, such as repeating structures and using vocabulary, though she occasionally closes up when things do not go her way. Her enthusiasm is evident in activities like the Dice Game and Family Bingo, where she contributes enthusiastically. While she may become upset in moments of frustration, such as when she could not see the Big Book or keep the blu-tack, she generally recovers and rejoins the activities. Her cooperative spirit is highlighted when she assists in distributing materials and participates in group tasks.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Mila has shown solid progress in acquiring vocabulary and structures, demonstrating a strong understanding of the material. She can retell stories with minimal assistance and combine moods with actions effectively. Her ability to pair sentences with weather items and use color vocabulary is commendable. However, she sometimes needs support with specific phrases, such as "taking off" and "putting on," and may struggle with routines when frustrated. Continued practice with these areas will help her build confidence and fluency.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally integrates well within the group and contributes positively to the classroom environment. She participates actively in activities and enjoys collaborative tasks, such as handing out materials and engaging in games. While she may become upset or disengaged when things do not go her way, she typically regains focus and rejoins the activities. Her behaviour during story time and structured tasks is generally good, though she occasionally needs reminders to stay engaged. Her mood can be affected by small frustrations, but she remains respectful and cooperative overall.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown strong engagement and enthusiasm in her learning this term. While she occasionally faces challenges with specific routines or frustrations, her overall progress in vocabulary and structure use is commendable. Continued practice with phrases and routines will support her further development. Keep up the great work, Mila!

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: [Teacher name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Camille demonstrates a positive and engaged attitude in class, particularly during activities she enjoys. She shows enthusiasm for interactive tasks such as A Present for Me, the Dice Game, and Family Bingo, where her participation is active and attentive. While she occasionally engages in playful distractions with peers—such as jokingly hiding materials—she generally responds well to encouragement and remains cooperative with the group. Her willingness to repeat vocabulary and structures when prompted reflects her openness to learning, though she sometimes hesitates when less confident. Camille’s motivation is strongest in collaborative and game-based activities, where she thrives and contributes positively to the classroom dynamic.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Camille has made notable progress in vocabulary retention and comprehension, particularly with colors, clothing items, and prepositions. She recalls parts of the story well and understands worksheet activities, such as identifying what characters can or cannot do. While she occasionally requires reminders for correct sentence structures, she demonstrates a solid grasp of the material and memorization of key vocabulary. Her ability to quickly adapt to new routines—such as miming weather and actions or describing moods with phrases like "he’s smiling because he’s happy"—shows her agility in learning. However, constructing questions about physical appearance before guessing remains a work in progress. Continued practice in forming complete questions and reinforcing structures will help her build further confidence.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Camille is generally well-integrated into the group and maintains a friendly demeanor with both peers and the teacher. Her behaviour is cooperative during activities, though she occasionally becomes playful in ways that briefly disrupt focus—such as jokingly withholding materials or engaging in side conversations with classmates like Zoé. These moments are minor and do not significantly detract from her overall positive contribution. She listens attentively during stories and new routines, and her mood is typically cheerful, even when she feels less confident. Gentle reminders help her refocus, and her ability to follow instructions and participate respectfully ensures a harmonious classroom environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has shown a commendable level of engagement and progress this term. Her enthusiasm for interactive activities and willingness to participate—even when challenging herself with new material—highlight her potential. While she occasionally needs encouragement to stay on task or to refine her use of structures, her positive attitude and growing vocabulary skills are strengths to build upon. Moving forward, continued practice in forming questions and maintaining focus during less preferred tasks will help her further develop her confidence and language abilities. With her cheerful disposition and cooperative nature, Camille is well-positioned to continue thriving in the classroom.

Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Try not to avoid repetition in the same section Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026
  • Absences: 0
  • Frequency of use (audio): Pass

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Oliver-level examples - accuracy: occasional playful behaviour noted without exaggeration
