response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: [Teacher name not provided in notes]
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Camille demonstrates a positive and engaged attitude in class, particularly during activities she enjoys. She shows enthusiasm for interactive tasks such as A Present for Me, the Dice Game, and Family Bingo, where her participation is active and attentive. While she occasionally engages in playful distractions with peers—such as jokingly hiding materials—she generally responds well to encouragement and remains cooperative with the group. Her willingness to repeat vocabulary and structures when prompted reflects her openness to learning, though she sometimes hesitates when less confident. Camille’s motivation is strongest in collaborative and game-based activities, where she thrives and contributes positively to the classroom dynamic.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Camille has made notable progress in vocabulary retention and comprehension, particularly with colors, clothing items, and prepositions. She recalls parts of the story well and understands worksheet activities, such as identifying what characters can or cannot do. While she occasionally requires reminders for correct sentence structures, she demonstrates a solid grasp of the material and memorization of key vocabulary. Her ability to quickly adapt to new routines—such as miming weather and actions or describing moods with phrases like "he’s smiling because he’s happy"—shows her agility in learning. However, constructing questions about physical appearance before guessing remains a work in progress. Continued practice in forming complete questions and reinforcing structures will help her build further confidence.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Camille is generally well-integrated into the group and maintains a friendly demeanor with both peers and the teacher. Her behaviour is cooperative during activities, though she occasionally becomes playful in ways that briefly disrupt focus—such as jokingly withholding materials or engaging in side conversations with classmates like Zoé. These moments are minor and do not significantly detract from her overall positive contribution. She listens attentively during stories and new routines, and her mood is typically cheerful, even when she feels less confident. Gentle reminders help her refocus, and her ability to follow instructions and participate respectfully ensures a harmonious classroom environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has shown a commendable level of engagement and progress this term. Her enthusiasm for interactive activities and willingness to participate—even when challenging herself with new material—highlight her potential. While she occasionally needs encouragement to stay on task or to refine her use of structures, her positive attitude and growing vocabulary skills are strengths to build upon. Moving forward, continued practice in forming questions and maintaining focus during less preferred tasks will help her further develop her confidence and language abilities. With her cheerful disposition and cooperative nature, Camille is well-positioned to continue thriving in the classroom.

```

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026
  • Absences: 0
  • Frequency of use (audio): Pass

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. She can recite parts of the story and demonstrates good memorization of the material. While she and the group needed reminders for correct structures during activities, she displayed a clear grasp of the worksheet tasks, such as identifying what Oliver and Baby could or couldn’t do. Her ability to adapt to new routines, like the mood routine with "he's smiling because he's happy," highlights her progress. Continued practice in forming questions about physical appearance will help her further develop her language skills.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Camille is generally attentive and integrates well within the group. She was occasionally playful with materials, jokingly hiding them, but this did not disrupt the class significantly. Her behavior was particularly praiseworthy during storytime, where she listened intently and enjoyed the new content. Though she showed some hesitation at times, her overall demeanor was positive, and she responded well to encouragement. Her interactions with classmates are friendly, and she contributes to a pleasant classroom atmosphere.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has had a productive term, demonstrating enthusiasm for classroom activities and a good grasp of vocabulary and routines. While she occasionally needs reminders to stay on task or return materials, her engagement and willingness to participate are strong. With continued practice, particularly in forming questions and using structures independently, she will further solidify her learning. Keep up the great work, Camille!

BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Try not to avoid repetition in the same section Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Camille
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver-Group 2 Saturday 2025/2026
  • Absences: 0
  • Frequency of use (audio): Pass

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Camille comes into class with a calm and positive attitude, and she shows a pleasant willingness to take part in the different activities proposed. She is generally attentive and open to new tasks, enjoying the story work, the worksheet activities and games such as Present for me, Who’s Missing and the Dice Game. Camille participates well when she feels comfortable, and she shows interest in the language being worked on, especially when she can recall vocabulary and repeat what she has learned. At times she can be a little more hesitant, but with encouragement she responds well and continues to engage positively with the group.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Camille has shown a good understanding of the story and the language structures worked on this term. She can remember vocabulary words very well, especially colours, clothing items and some prepositions, and she also demonstrated understanding in activities about what Oliver and Baby could or could not do. She was able to recite parts of the story and to repeat when asked, showing that she is memorising the language and building her confidence. She sometimes needs help to recall the correct structures, particularly when asking questions or using the new language independently, but she shows real understanding and is making steady progress. Continued practice will help her become more secure and more confident when using the structures spontaneously.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Very good

Comment:
Camille has a peaceful, easy-going and attentive manner in class. She is generally well integrated within the group and her calm presence helps create a positive atmosphere. She is usually focused and listens carefully, showing a good level of concentration during activities. On occasion she can be a little playful with the materials or slightly distracted with a classmate, but this does not affect her overall behaviour, which remains very good. Camille responds well to guidance, follows the classroom routine and shows a kind and cooperative attitude towards both the teacher and her peers.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.10  Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________
Camille has had a positive term and has shown good understanding of the story content and vocabulary worked on in class. Her calm and attentive nature, together with her willingness to participate, have contributed well to the group. She is progressing nicely and, with continued practice and confidence, she will keep developing her language skills next term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The student profile is calm, attentive, and generally positive — the report should maintain a warm and encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Oliver-level examples - accuracy: occasional playful behaviour noted without exaggeration

response.output:
Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Camille
Teacher: [Teacher Name]
Level: Oliver 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Camille demonstrates a positive attitude towards classroom activities, showing enthusiasm particularly during engaging tasks like the worksheet activity about what Oliver and Baby could or couldn’t do. She participates willingly in group activities, such as the Dice Game and "A present for me," and enjoys contributing to the learning environment. While she occasionally requires reminders to maintain focus, her willingness to engage and cooperate with peers and the teacher is evident. Her enjoyment of new activities, such as the weather and action miming routine, further highlights her motivation to participate.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Camille has shown a solid understanding of vocabulary related to clothing, colors, and prepositions, demonstrating good memorisation skills. She can recite parts of the story and actively participates in activities like the worksheet task and "Who’s missing." While she occasionally needs support to recall structures, her comprehension and application of previously acquired knowledge are evident. Her ability to quickly grasp new routines, such as the weather and mood activities, indicates a strong learning process. Continued reinforcement of structures will further enhance her confidence and proficiency.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Camille generally exhibits positive behaviour in the classroom, interacting kindly with peers and participating enthusiastically in group activities. She enjoys collaborative tasks like Family Bingo and the Dice Game, contributing to a positive group dynamic. While she occasionally becomes distracted or playful with materials, her overall behaviour is appropriate, and she responds well to encouragement. Her attentiveness during storytelling and new routines reflects her ability to engage appropriately in the learning environment.

Final comment

____________________________________________________________

Camille has shown a strong commitment to her learning this term, participating actively and demonstrating enthusiasm for a variety of activities. Her ability to recall vocabulary and engage in group tasks highlights her progress, though continued practice with structures will support her further development. With her positive attitude and willingness to participate, Camille is well-positioned to build on her achievements in the future.

- 2026-05-14: Hugo was attentive and motivated as always. Hugo and Nina had forgotten how to use connectors such as although and despite. Hugo still makes some pronunciation mistakes and sometimes makes tense mistakes like “didn’t found.” The class could do the exercises quite well and understand the new vocabulary very well too, and they all did an effort to express themselves.
- 2026-05-14: They could all express themselves rather well when talking about food groups, dishes and meals. Hugo made a couple of grammatical mistakes, like using a verb in -ing after could, and he did some spelling mistakes, but he could correct them almost all directly when they were pointed out to him. - 2026-05-14: Hugo could answer questions using the target language quite well. COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Hugo
Teacher: Hélène Marchal
Level: Fairy Tales 2 Tuesday & Friday
Absences: 0

---

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent
Comment:

Hugo consistently demonstrates a positive and motivated attitude in class. He is attentive, engaged, and always willing to participate enthusiastically in activities. His polite and cooperative nature contributes to a supportive classroom environment, and he actively listens and asks for clarification when needed. Hugo responds well to feedback and corrections, showing a genuine eagerness to improve and learn.

---

Work

Assessment (Work): Good
Comment:

Hugo works diligently and remains committed to his studies. He actively participates in discussions and approaches learning with curiosity. He completes tasks seriously and takes initiative in correcting his mistakes, often self-correcting without prompting. His responsible attitude and willingness to assist others demonstrate strong engagement with the subject. However, he occasionally struggles with certain grammatical structures and pronunciation, which he works to improve.

---

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good
Comment:

Hugo performs well in class activities and assessments, demonstrating a solid understanding of the material. He can express himself using the target language and structures effectively, though he occasionally makes minor grammatical or pronunciation errors. He reads and summarizes chapters well, uses vocabulary appropriately, and engages confidently in oral exercises. His ability to correct himself and incorporate feedback shows strong progress.

---

Final comment

Throughout the term, Hugo has shown consistent dedication and enthusiasm in his learning. His positive attitude, respectful behaviour, and strong participation have contributed positively to the classroom environment. While he occasionally faces challenges with pronunciation, grammar, or specific vocabulary, his willingness to correct himself and seek clarification demonstrates his commitment to improvement. With continued focus on refining grammatical accuracy and expanding vocabulary, Hugo is well-positioned to further strengthen his language skills. Over the summer, he can reinforce his progress by practising speaking, reviewing grammar structures, and engaging with English texts to build confidence and fluency for the next academic year.

The student notes are the sole source of facts for the report.
Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples.

The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary,
section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided
additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence.
IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples —
only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes.

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Hugo
  • Class: Fairy Tales 2 (Tuesday & Friday)

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent

Comment:
Hugo is a motivated and attentive student who consistently brings a positive attitude to class. He is always calm, polite, and happy to participate, creating a welcoming atmosphere for both classmates and the teacher. His enthusiasm for activities—such as the Dice Game, drawing, and explaining expressions—demonstrates his engagement and enjoyment of the learning process. Hugo is highly cooperative, willingly accepts corrections, and actively seeks clarification when unsure. His supportive nature, including helping peers like Nina with spelling, further enriches the classroom environment.

Work

Assessment (Work): Very good

Comment:
Hugo approaches his work with dedication and seriousness. He completes exercises thoroughly, reviews key concepts like articles in texts, and participates eagerly in all activities. His ability to summarize chapters, form questions, and use target language structures—such as "would rather…than…" and "prefer…to…"—reflects his strong effort and responsibility. While he occasionally requires inspiration or guidance, such as clarifying the purpose of connectors, he consistently demonstrates a willingness to improve. His attentive and fun-loving attitude makes him a pleasure to teach.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Good

Comment:
Hugo shows a solid understanding of the material, particularly in reading, listening, and using grammatical structures. He performs well in exercises, fills in gaps accurately during listening tasks, and can report characters' dialogue effectively. His knowledge of vocabulary and sentence formation is good, though he sometimes struggles with pronunciation (e.g., repeating corrected words) and persistent errors, such as omitting the 's' in third-person singular verbs or misusing articles. While he occasionally makes tense mistakes (e.g., "didn’t found") or spelling errors (e.g., "wich" for "which"), he nearly always corrects himself when prompted or realizes his mistakes independently. His ability to discuss topics like food groups, school rules, and music demonstrates growing fluency, though he benefits from occasional support to expand his vocabulary usage.

Marks

Oral Performance:
Hugo communicates with increasing confidence and clarity. His pronunciation has improved, though occasional mistakes persist, particularly with verb endings and articles. He expresses himself well in structured tasks, such as riddles and discussions about stories, and shows a strong ability to self-correct. To further enhance his fluency, he should focus on internalizing connectors (e.g., "although," "despite") and expanding his active vocabulary. Regular practice in spontaneous speaking will help him reduce hesitation and refine his accuracy.

Written Performance:
Hugo demonstrates a good grasp of grammar and sentence structure in writing. He can construct questions and answers using target language effectively, and his work in gapped sentences and summaries is commendable. However, spelling errors (e.g., "wich") and grammatical slips (e.g., verbs in -ing after "could") occasionally affect his accuracy. With continued attention to proofreading and revising key rules—such as third-person singular forms—he can achieve even greater precision. His willingness to review and correct mistakes positions him well for progress.

Final comment

Hugo has made notable progress this term, showcasing his motivation, responsibility, and positive attitude in every class. His strengths lie in his enthusiasm for participation, his ability to self-correct, and his growing confidence in both oral and written tasks. While pronunciation and persistent grammatical errors (e.g., verb endings, articles) remain areas for development, his attentive nature and eagerness to learn ensure steady improvement. To build on his achievements, Hugo should focus on regular practice of connectors and vocabulary application, as well as mindful review of recurring mistakes. Over the summer, engaging with English through reading, listening to songs, and conversational practice will help him consolidate his skills and prepare confidently for the next level. His cooperative spirit and joyful presence continue to make him a valued member of the class.

plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. This tests whether a
second student in the same class produces a comparable-quality report. The student
is strong across all dimensions with consistent motivation and self-correction
habits. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Marks, Homework marks, Absences,
Audio frequency, or other numerical metadata) — only include facts traceable to
the source notes. Score 1-5 on:
- structure: three sections (Behaviour/Work/Performance) + Final comment
- grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no
  fabricated homework or test marks)
- tone: constructive and encouraging, matches Fairy Tales 2 examples
- consistency: quality comparable to reference; specific weaknesses (pronunciation,
  spelling, grammar) acknowledged accurately

Every observation, data field, and mark must come from the notes — not from any examples.

## Style & Layout Guide
The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary,
section headings, and layout exactly. These examples may differ slightly from the provided
additional instructions - in that case, the instructions from the user take precedence.
IMPORTANT: Do not copy specific Data field values, Marks, or observations from the examples —
only include a Data field or Marks section if that value is present in the student notes.

- 2026-05-14: Hugo was calm and attentive. He did the work he was asked and did it as well as he could. He could read the chapter quite well, without pronunciation mistakes, but he was not totally fluent. He could summarize the chapters using the target language written on the board rather well, make questions from the target language, answer using it too, and understand a song and answer questions about it. He could form a question and an answer using “would rather…than…” and “prefer… to…”. He could not really use vocabulary words and make sentences with them that showed understanding of the words.
- 2026-05-14: Hugo could answer questions using the target language quite well. They could do the listening activity quite well and could report what the characters had said quite well, although I needed to tell Hugo to mind the ‘s’ at the 3rd person singular. Clara and Hugo struggled a bit with the grammar revision, but still enjoyed the game. Their game revision of the vocabulary was better. Hugo’s pronunciation was alright and he always takes my remarks into account. He also helped Nina spell 'teen' for her My Talent task.
- 2026-05-14: Hugo could do the reading exercise and fill in the blanks with the listening exercise very, very well, and he guessed almost all of the words. That was great. He also did well in the oral exercises, and even though he still sometimes makes mistakes, he can almost always correct himself, and sometimes he realizes it and corrects them directly without needing my help. He had a harder time guessing the meaning of some words from my definitions, matching them.
- 2026-05-14: Hugo took part in the activities quite well and could use the target language in the gapped sentences quite well. He still makes some mistakes when he speaks, but he always tries to correct himself, and when I point out what he needs to correct, he does it. They could do the grammar activity very well and seemed to have fun doing the games too.
- 2026-05-14: Hugo only made a few mistakes, very few mistakes, when he had to express himself orally. He showed quite a good knowledge of the grammatical structures we've seen so far and could talk about the story and the previous chapters quite well, using some of the target language.
- 2026-05-14: Hugo was very motivated. He made some mistakes, usually the same ones: he forgets to use the s at the end of verbs when he talks in the third person singular, and he sometimes makes mistakes with articles, but he can always correct himself. He can also speak fluently about topics like music, and when he doesn't understand something, he can ask for clarification. He was in a lovely mood, and it was lovely to have him in class.

## Task
Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above.
Output the report as clean HTML (using <p>, <h3>, <ul>, <li> tags as appropriate).
Do not include <html>, <head>, or <body> wrapper tags — just the content HTML.

values are explicitly present in the notes. Do not invent or carry over values from the examples.
Every statement in the report must be traceable to the notes. Do not invent observations.
Follow the tone, section headings, and layout of the examples exactly.
Do not replicate their specific Data field values, Marks, or observations —
the report content comes only from the student notes above.

response.output:
Kids&Us.

COURSE OBJECTIVES - FAIRY TALES 2, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Hugo
Teacher: Hélène Marchal
Level: Fairy Tales 2 2025/2026
Class: Tuesday & Friday

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Excellent
Comment:
Hugo is a motivated, attentive, and enthusiastic student. He consistently maintains a positive attitude in class, participating actively and happily in all activities. He is polite, cooperative, and always willing to correct his mistakes when pointed out. His eagerness to engage and his supportive nature contribute to a productive and enjoyable learning environment.

Work

Assessment (Work): Very good
Comment:
Hugo works diligently and demonstrates a strong commitment to his studies. He completes exercises seriously, engages in discussions, and shows a good understanding of the material. He is always willing to review and correct his work, and he takes feedback into account. His ability to summarize chapters, form questions, and use target language structures reflects his dedication and effort.

Performance

Assessment (Performance): Very good
Comment:
Hugo performs well in assessments, showing a solid grasp of vocabulary, grammar, and comprehension. He can express himself clearly in oral tasks, though he occasionally makes pronunciation and grammatical mistakes, such as omitting the 's' in third-person singular verbs or misusing articles. However, he is quick to self-correct or adjust his responses when guided. His ability to understand and apply target language structures, as well as his strong performance in listening and reading exercises, demonstrates his progress.

---

Final comment

Hugo has shown consistent motivation and engagement throughout the term. His positive attitude, willingness to correct mistakes, and active participation have contributed to his strong performance. While he occasionally struggles with pronunciation and certain grammatical structures, his ability to self-correct and apply feedback is commendable. With continued focus on refining these areas, Hugo is well-positioned to further improve his language skills. Over the summer, he should practice speaking, review grammar rules, and expand his vocabulary to build confidence for the next academic year.
